artifacts.content:
From: emily.thompson@lincolnmsisd.org
To: javier.ortiz@lincolnmsisd.org
Subject: Brain dump before we disappear into in-service
Date: October 7, 2025

Hey Javier,

Typing this while Noah is “reading” a Dog Man graphic novel to Lily and really just making up half the words. It’s kind of perfect background noise for thinking about StoryLabs.

I’ve been reflecting a lot on why I’m so stubborn about keeping our inclusive groups front and center in this project. I keep going back to two things:

1) That awful PLC in the Lincoln library last October when Mr. Jennings called our unit “fluffy tech stuff” and said, in front of everyone, that “those IEP kids should be in a resource room, not coding games.” I still can’t believe he said that out loud. I was so rattled I ended up emailing Elena Rodriguez that night to file an informal complaint, because it felt like more than just grumpiness – it was flat-out dismissive of our kids.

2) The day during spring 2025 benchmarks when I left the stack of accommodations lists on the cart outside Room 214. Fifteen minutes, tops, but it felt like an eternity. When your text popped up – subject line “Accommodations list in hall” – my stomach dropped. I grabbed them before the kids came back from lunch, but ever since then I’ve been hyper‑paranoid about student privacy, especially as we start having them log into different coding platforms and share projects.

Between that and my own little meltdown last April in 7th‑period ELA (the panic episode where I stepped into the hallway by 214 to catch my breath), it’s pretty clear my brain is carrying a lot around this work. I’m honestly grateful I started seeing Dr. Lauren McKay over at the Denton ISD Support Center in June 2024. The twice‑a‑month sessions have been the only reason I didn’t completely burn out while we piloted this.

Anyway, practical stuff: I mocked up a draft timeline from the Sept. 2, 2025 start through the Interactive Story Showcase Night in May 2026. I built in extra revision time for our English learners and kids with IEPs, plus a couple of after‑school workshop days for parents/teachers so families can actually see the coding side.

Remind me tomorrow to ask you about platforms licenses and whether we can still swing a classroom set of durable student laptops with about $7,450. I’m trying to be realistic.

See you at PD (I’ll bring coffee),
Emily

metadata.subject: Brain dump before we disappear into in-service

artifacts.content:
From: emily.thompson@lincolnmsisd.org
To: samantha.pierce@uta.edu
Subject: Sunday check-in + kid chaos report
Date: March 23, 2025

Hey Sam,

Your Sunday evening nag text is arriving right on schedule. Consider this my official “how are you, really?” poke.

I’ve been thinking a lot about that Thanksgiving 2023 conversation in Plano when you told me about the spring 2023 semester at UT Arlington – the passive suicidal thoughts, and how you ended up meeting every week with Dr. Renee Alvarez in those counseling offices over in College Park Center. I’m still so proud of you for getting help and sticking with it. That’s honestly part of why I keep texting you every Sunday night, even when I’m half-asleep grading.

On my end, the emotional circus continues. After that panic spell outside Room 214 last April during 7th‑period ELA – when I had to step into the hallway because my heart was racing and my hands were shaking – I finally admitted I couldn’t white‑knuckle my way through the year. I started seeing Dr. Lauren McKay at the Denton ISD Support Center in June 2024, twice a month. She’s been helping me untangle the anxiety and burnout from juggling big inclusive classes and this STEM StoryLabs thing I’m co‑leading.

Family updates: Mom is doing pretty well post‑heart procedure. You remember the scare – when she briefly lost consciousness in the produce aisle at the Plano Costco and then ended up at Medical City Plano May 3–6, 2025 for that minor procedure with Dr. Patel. I set up a shared Google Calendar called “Mom – Dr. Patel appts” so we can track her cardiology follow‑ups. I’ve been driving up to Plano one Saturday a month for groceries and meds runs. If you want access to the calendar, I can share it to your UTA email.

Meanwhile, Noah is obsessed with Minecraft and Lily is currently teaching her stuffed animals how to “code” on a cardboard laptop. Mark is…Mark. We’re both tired.

Write back when you have the bandwidth. Even a two‑line “I’m okay” counts.

Love you,
Em

Title: StoryLabs – why this matters (for me)
Date: November 2, 2025

I keep coming back to why I’m pouring so much of myself into STEM StoryLabs: “Integrating Coding and Creative Writing Through Interactive Narratives.” It’s not just a cute title.

First, the kids. We’re talking about roughly 128 students across my 7th‑grade ELA sections and the after‑school STEM StoryLabs club – grades 6–8 technically, but mostly 7th. About 40% on free/reduced lunch, 18% receiving special education services, and 22% current or former English learners. So many reading below grade level but lighting up the second we mention games, digital media, or graphic novels.

Second, my own journey. Back in March 2019 at Texas Woman’s University, I got that “Academic Integrity Concern – EDTC 5323” email from Professor Daniel Hsu for reusing a chunk of a prior lesson plan in my Digital Pedagogy course without clearly citing myself in the portfolio. It was technically self‑plagiarism, but it shook me. Since then I’ve been almost paranoid about crediting platforms, authors, and even student ideas when we build materials.

Add to that the financial reality: in February 2025, Mark and I had to switch my remaining ~$37,000 M.Ed. student loans over to an income‑driven repayment plan with FedLoan Servicing after we fell a month behind in late 2024. The missed payment happened right after that surprise $1,800 repair on our 2013 Honda CR‑V at Denton Auto Clinic. There’s no way my families – or we – can just “chip in” for devices.

So when I talk about needing a classroom set of durable student laptops, graphic novels as mentor texts, and licenses for kid‑friendly coding/story platforms, it’s because this is the only way many of my students will get to build something interactive that feels like theirs.

Note to self: keep emphasizing the cycle – brainstorming, drafting, peer review, coding branching narratives, user testing, revision, and then the Interactive Story Showcase Night for parents and teachers in May 2026.

From: emily.thompson@lincolnmsisd.org
To: mark.thompson@gmail.com
Subject: Can we talk about October nights?
Date: September 5, 2024

Hey M,

I didn’t want our only conversation about this to be that argument in the Oak Ridge Elementary parking lot last month, with Noah half‑asleep in the backseat and Lily clutching her stuffed bunny. Hearing you say I “care more about those StoryLabs kids than Noah and Lily” in front of the Honda CR‑V – and realizing Mrs. Karen Lewis was close enough to overhear – stung more than I could get out in the moment.

I get why you’re frustrated. Three extra after‑school club nights in October means more evenings where you’re solo for dinner, homework, and bath. But this isn’t just “extra credit” for me. The STEM StoryLabs stuff is woven into my actual 7th‑grade ELA work now – reading, writing, media literacy, the whole thing – and it’s one of the few parts of my job that still feels energizing instead of draining.

You know how rough last spring was. That afternoon during 7th‑period ELA when I had the panic episode outside Room 214 and had to step into the hallway to catch my breath? That was my wake‑up call. I’ve been meeting with Dr. Lauren McKay at the Denton ISD Support Center twice a month since June 2024 for anxiety and burnout. She keeps reminding me that I’m allowed to care deeply about my students without it meaning I don’t care about my own kids.

Logistics-wise, I’ll make those October nights as painless as possible. On club days I can grab takeout on the way home so you’re not cooking on top of everything else. We can also block off one Saturday a month as a non‑negotiable family day – no school email, no charting for you.

Can we sit down after the kids are in bed tonight and actually map out October and November together? I don’t want resentment to build up on either side.

Love,
E

metadata.subject: Can we talk about October nights?

artifacts.content:
Title: Teacher brain dump – boundaries + coping
Date: January 28, 2025

I need to be honest with myself about how I’m coping with this job right now.

1. Counseling: Since June 2024 I’ve been seeing Dr. Lauren McKay at the Denton ISD Support Center twice a month for anxiety and burnout. That April 2024 panic episode in Room 214 during 7th‑period ELA – when I had to step into the hallway because I thought I might pass out – was the tipping point. She keeps reminding me that big inclusive classes plus the STEM StoryLabs pilot would be a lot for anyone.

2. Social media venting: In January 2025 I created that private, locked Instagram account, @quietroom214. Only Javier and Megan from UNT know about it. I never use student names, but I do post anonymized, venting captions about teaching – like the one after that parent‑teacher conference where a Lincoln Middle parent accused me of “ruining reading with screens.” I know it’s risky and probably not what Denton ISD’s social media policy would love, but it’s been a pressure valve.

3. Colleagues: I’m still angry about what Mr. Jennings said in the Lincoln Middle School library during the October 2024 PLC – calling StoryLabs “fluffy tech stuff” and saying “those IEP kids should be in a resource room, not coding games.” I ended up emailing assistant principal Elena Rodriguez that night to file an informal complaint. That moment is burned into my brain as a reminder of why I’m doing inclusive, rigorous work with my kids.

4. Boundaries: I need actual rules: no school email after 8:30 p.m., at least one fully work‑free Saturday a month (even with the monthly Plano run for Mom), and no posting on @quietroom214 if I’m more than a 6/10 on the anger scale.

This year could be a turning point if I don’t burn myself out first.

Event: Monthly Plano run – Mom – Dr. Patel follow-up + groceries
Date: Saturday, June 7, 2025
Time: 9:00 a.m. – 5:30 p.m.
Location: Medical City Plano / Costco Plano / Carolyn & David’s house

Notes:

– 9:00–11:30 a.m.: Drive from Denton to Plano with kids. Bring snacks, tablets, and Noah’s Minecraft backpack. Pick up Mom at the house.

– 12:00–1:15 p.m.: Cardiology follow‑up with Dr. Patel at Medical City Plano. This is one of the ongoing checks after her minor heart procedure May 3–6, 2025, which happened after she briefly lost consciousness in the produce aisle at the Plano Costco. Bring updated med list and questions about dizziness.

– 1:30–3:00 p.m.: Costco Plano run for groceries and household stuff. Try not to let Dad buy another industrial‑sized jar of pickles. Watch Mom closely in the store – no wandering off alone in produce.

– 3:15–4:30 p.m.: Stop by Target for Lily’s preschool snacks and a small prize for Noah if he helps with Grandma. Quick coffee for me.

– 4:30–5:30 p.m.: Drive back to Denton. Kids will probably crash in the car.

Reminder to self: update the shared Google Calendar “Mom – Dr. Patel appts” so Sam can see this. Also factor these first‑Saturday trips into planning for after‑school STEM StoryLabs workshops and any Interactive Story Showcase Night dates so I’m not double‑booking myself.

metadata.title: Monthly Plano run – Mom – Dr. Patel follow-up + groceries

artifacts.content:
From: emily.thompson@lincolnmsisd.org
To: megan.carter@email.com
Subject: I accidentally became “the coding ELA person”
Date: February 18, 2025

Hey Meg,

Catching up from my couch while Mark watches some game I don’t understand.

Somehow I’ve become “the coding ELA person” at Lincoln Middle. The STEM StoryLabs thing has grown from a tiny after‑school club into this whole year‑long experience where my 7th graders write and code interactive stories and simple games. It’s awesome and exhausting.

You’d laugh at how full my plate is. We’re serving about 128 kids across grades 6–8 (mostly my 7th‑grade ELA sections), and a big chunk are struggling readers, English learners, or students with IEPs. They’re obsessed with games and digital media, so when I bring in high‑interest graphic novels as mentor texts and then let them build branching narratives, they forget they “hate reading.”

The grown‑up side is less magical. Money is tight at home – remember how in late 2024 our 2013 Honda CR‑V died on the highway and the Denton Auto Clinic bill came to about $1,800? That’s when we slipped a month behind on my grad school loans. In February 2025 we finally switched my remaining ~$37,000 M.Ed. balance over to an income‑driven repayment plan with FedLoan Servicing. So when people suggest I just “fundraise for laptops,” I want to scream. Most of my families are in the same boat.

On top of that, I’m still cringing about that day during spring benchmarks when I left the printed accommodations lists – with student names, IEP statuses, and supports – sitting on a cart in the hallway outside Room 214 for like 15 minutes. Javier texted me “Accommodations list in hall” and I grabbed them before kids came back from lunch, but wow, FERPA nightmares.

Coping mechanisms: lots of coffee, the occasional vent on my locked @quietroom214 Insta (no names, I promise), and reading trashy romance on my phone after the kids are asleep.

Tell me grad school at UNT is less chaotic than my middle schoolers.

Love,
Em

metadata.subject: I accidentally became “the coding ELA person”

artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

I hope you're feeling well and recovering smoothly since the procedure. I wanted to update you on a few exciting developments both at work and home.

First off, wonderful news! I recently received a grant of $7,450 to support our STEM StoryLabs Club at Lincoln Middle School. This will allow Javier and me to purchase new tech tools and resources for the students, which should make a big difference as we prepare for the Interactive Story Showcase Night next May. I can’t wait to see the kids’ faces when they realize what they can create with the new equipment.

At home, things are buzzing with the usual activity. Mark is busy with his work at Denton Sports & Rehab, but he’s been stepping up to help more with the kids' morning routine. Noah is excited to start second grade at Oak Ridge Elementary. He's been obsessed with his Minecraft backpack, and I think he might even be more excited about showing it off than starting school! Lily, meanwhile, is ready for Bright Futures Preschool. She’s been 'teaching' her dolls how to code stories, which is both adorable and a bit of foreshadowing, I think!

We’ve settled quite nicely into our home on Greenbriar Lane since buying it in 2021. It’s hard to believe how much housing prices have jumped since then. It’s a relief we managed to buy when we did. Still, money is a bit tight, with daycare and student loans from my M.Ed. nibbling away at our budget, not to mention the occasional hiccups with the old Honda CR-V. But we’re managing.

I’ll be coming to Plano on the 28th for our usual grocery run and tech help session. I’ll bring Noah and Lily along so they can spend some time with you and Dad. Please let me know if there’s anything specific you need help with or if you’d like me to bring anything special.

Looking forward to seeing you soon!

Love,
Emily
